prevalence.trend: increasing in urban areas with air pollution

burden.mortalityRate: Low but higher where controller access is limited

vulnerablePopulations: urban children exposed to high PM2.5
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis with recurrent wheeze/cough; spirometry where available; response to bronchodilator/controller therapy; rule out TB and other causes

publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Low to moderate due to inhaler access gaps and adherence issues

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Often clinical; spirometry limited; many treated intermittently
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: ICS controller therapy, SABA reliever; education and trigger avoidance; referral for severe asthma
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High air pollution, poor inhaler adherence, and limited access to specialist pediatric pulmonology
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Availability of affordable generic inhalers and expanding child health services
